expanded_description: Coordinate geometry provides a systematic algebraic method for classifying quadrilaterals. By placing a quadrilateral on the coordinate plane and using the distance formula, slope formula, and midpoint formula, you can determine whether it is a parallelogram, rectangle, rhombus, square, trapezoid, isosceles trapezoid, or kite. Each quadrilateral type has specific algebraic signatures: parallel lines have equal slopes, perpendicular lines have negative reciprocal slopes, congruent segments have equal distances, and bisected segments share the same midpoint.

core_concept.principle: A quadrilateral is classified using three formulas. Slope: equal slopes → parallel lines; negative reciprocal slopes → perpendicular lines. Distance: equal distances → congruent segments. Midpoint: same midpoint for two diagonals → they bisect each other. By testing these properties in the right order, any quadrilateral can be classified with certainty.
core_concept.explanation: Instead of relying on how a quadrilateral looks—which can be deceptive—coordinate geometry lets you calculate exactly what it is. Each quadrilateral type has a unique combination of algebraic conditions. Start with the most general (is it a trapezoid? a parallelogram?) and work toward the most specific (is it a rhombus? a rectangle? a square?). Every conclusion is backed by a numerical calculation, not a visual guess.

image.alt: A coordinate plane with a quadrilateral plotted. Three tool icons: slope, distance, midpoint. Flowchart from general to specific classification.
image.caption: Coordinate classification: slope, distance, and midpoint formulas determine the quadrilateral type.

sections.introduction: Three algebraic formulas test specific geometric properties. Using them in combination allows precise identification of any quadrilateral type.

introduction_image.alt: Quadrilateral on a grid. Three formulas displayed: slope, distance, midpoint. Arrows to properties: parallel/perpendicular, congruent, bisect.
introduction_image.caption: Three formulas, three properties: slope, distance, midpoint.

key_concept.definition: The three essential formulas: (1) Slope: $m = (y_2-y_1)/(x_2-x_1)$. Equal slopes → parallel. Product = −1 → perpendicular. (2) Distance: $d = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2+(y_2-y_1)^2}$. Equal distances → congruent segments. (3) Midpoint: $M = ((x_1+x_2)/2, (y_1+y_2)/2)$. Same midpoint for both diagonals → they bisect each other.
key_concept.context: These formulas are the algebraic translations of geometric properties. Slope captures direction. Distance captures length. Midpoint captures bisection. Computing these for sides and diagonals verifies every geometric relationship numerically.

image.alt: Each formula with a geometric diagram: slope with parallel/perpendicular lines, distance with congruent segments, midpoint with bisecting diagonals.
image.caption: Slope → direction. Distance → length. Midpoint → bisection.

process_flow.process: Plot and Label Vertices in Order
process_flow.description: Place the quadrilateral on the coordinate plane. Label vertices in clockwise or counterclockwise order.
process_flow.mechanism: Given A(x₁,y₁), B(x₂,y₂), C(x₃,y₃), D(x₄,y₄) in order. Opposite sides: AB and CD, BC and DA. Diagonals: AC and BD.
process_flow.result: Vertices ordered; opposite sides and diagonals identified.

image.alt: Quadrilateral plotted with vertices labeled in order.
image.caption: Step 1: Plot and label vertices in order.

process_flow.process: Compute Slopes of All Four Sides
process_flow.description: Find the slope of each side. Compare slopes to identify parallel and perpendicular relationships.
process_flow.mechanism: If m_AB = m_CD → AB ∥ CD. If m_BC = m_DA → BC ∥ DA. Both pairs parallel → parallelogram. Exactly one pair parallel → trapezoid. Adjacent slopes with product −1 → perpendicular.
process_flow.result: Parallel/perpendicular relationships determined. Parallelogram or trapezoid identified.
applications: AB slope = 1/4, CD slope = 1/4 → AB ∥ CD. BC slope = 4, DA slope = 4 → BC ∥ DA. Both pairs → parallelogram.

image.alt: Quadrilateral with slope calculations. Parallel pairs highlighted.
image.caption: Step 2: Compute slopes—equal slopes indicate parallel lines.

process_flow.process: Compute Side Lengths and Check Diagonals
process_flow.description: Use distance formula for side lengths. Use midpoint and distance on diagonals.
process_flow.mechanism: Parallelogram: all sides equal → rhombus. Adjacent perpendicular → rectangle. Both → square. Diagonals: same midpoint → bisect (parallelogram). Equal length → congruent (rectangle or isosceles trapezoid).
applications: $AB = BC = \sqrt{17}$ → all sides equal → rhombus. Slopes not perpendicular → not rectangle. Classification: rhombus.

image.alt: Distance and midpoint calculations. Diagonal comparison.
image.caption: Step 3: Use distances and midpoints to refine classification.

classifications.value: Each quadrilateral type has a unique combination of algebraic conditions.
characteristics: Parallelogram: both pairs opposite sides parallel (slopes equal).
Rectangle: parallelogram + adjacent sides perpendicular (slope product = −1).
Rhombus: parallelogram + all four sides congruent (equal distances).
Square: rectangle + rhombus (all sides equal AND right angles).
Trapezoid: exactly one pair opposite sides parallel.
Isosceles trapezoid: trapezoid + congruent legs or congruent diagonals.
Kite: two pairs adjacent congruent sides + perpendicular diagonals.

examples.explanation: Slopes first → how many parallel pairs? None → kite or general. One → trapezoid. Two → parallelogram. Then distances → all sides equal? Adjacent perpendicular? Classify as rhombus, rectangle, or square.

image.alt: Flowchart from slopes through distances to final classification.
image.caption: Systematic classification: slopes, then distances, then diagonals.

image.alt: Complete toolkit with formulas, flowchart, and algebraic signatures.
image.caption: Classifying Quadrilaterals in the Coordinate Plane overview.

sections.introduction: Apply the coordinate toolkit to classify quadrilaterals from given vertices.

worked_examples.title: Coordinate Classification Problems

step_by_step.explanation: AB ∥ CD (slope 0), BC ∥ DA (slope 3). Parallelogram. AB=4, BC=√10, not equal, not perpendicular. Parallelogram only.
Opposite sides parallel (horizontal and vertical). Adjacent perpendicular → rectangle. Sides 6 and 4, not equal → not square. Rectangle.
AB ∥ CD (slope 0). BC slope ≠ DA slope. Exactly one pair parallel → trapezoid. Legs √17 and √20, not congruent → not isosceles.
Opposite sides parallel. Adjacent perpendicular. All sides = 5. Square.

image.alt: Four quadrilaterals with calculations and classifications.
image.caption: Coordinate classification practice.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Slope, distance, and midpoint formulas for quadrilateral classification.

summary: Coordinate geometry classifies quadrilaterals using slope (parallel/perpendicular), distance (congruence), and midpoint (bisection). Follow a logical order: slopes first, then distances, then diagonals. Each quadrilateral type has a unique algebraic signature.

review_questions_image.caption: Classifying Quadrilaterals in the Coordinate Plane Review.
